actions.thought: QTo is a strong opening hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to assert control and leverage my position.

KJo is a solid starting hand for a 3-handed game. Defending the small blind against a button open is standard, even with the positional disadvantage. I have sufficient pot odds to call and see a flop.

With A9 suited in the Big Blind facing a Button raise and a Small Blind call, I am getting 3.5 to 1 pot odds to close the action. A9s is too strong to fold here. While a squeeze is an option, calling is highly profitable given the price, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to realize my hand's post-flop potential with nut flush draw and good top pair capabilities.

The board texture of 2d-6s-7s is very low and connected, which hits my opponents' ranges much harder than my K-J offsuit. With no pair and no significant draw, betting here would be a bluff into two opponents, which is high variance and unlikely to succeed. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on their actions while keeping the pot small.

We flopped the nut flush draw with an overcard on a low, somewhat connected board (2d 6s 7s). Being out of position multiway, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard line. This allows us to either see a free card if it checks through, or check-raise to build the pot and apply fold equity if the Button c-bets. Donk betting could fold out worse hands but might isolate us against stronger ranges without gaining much information. Checking gives us the most flexibility.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a three-way pot. The board is 2d-6s-7s, which is relatively coordinated and offers several draws. While a continuation bet could take the pot down now, checking back allows me to see a turn for free with my Queen-high and control the pot size. In a three-way scenario, my range advantage is slightly diluted, and I don't want to get check-raised off my equity. I will check and re-evaluate on the turn.

The board (2d-6s-7s-3c) is very low and connected, which strongly favors the calling ranges of my opponents rather than my King-high hand. Since I have no pair, no draw, and zero showdown value, betting would be an unnecessary bluff into two opponents. Checking is the most strategic play to keep the pot size manageable and potentially see a free river card, or safely fold if a bet is made by an opponent.

The flop checked through, indicating weakness from both opponents. On the turn, the Small Blind checks again. With the nut flush draw and an overcard, this is a prime opportunity to probe. Leading out allows me to take the initiative, build the pot for when I hit my flush, and generate fold equity to potentially win the pot right now against weak pairs or better Ace-highs.

I have Queen-high with no draw on a board of 2d-6s-7s-3c. Pro led for 500 into a 900 pot after the flop was checked around. Given the board texture and the fact that I have zero showdown value or significant draws, calling or raising would be overplaying my hand. I will fold and preserve my stack.

The board is 2d-6s-7s-3c. My Kh-Jd is effectively pure air at this point. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Pro has bet 500 into a 900 pot, and with three players involved, the likelihood that my King-high is ahead is extremely low. There is no strategic reason to call a bet with no showdown value on such a coordinated low-card board. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for a better spot.

4-9 offsuit is a weak, unplayable hand with poor equity against both blinds. Even though I have the advantage of being on the button, this hand lacks the strength to warrant a raise or a call at this stage of the tournament. I will fold to conserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

With Q7 suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a great opportunity to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 3x (300) allows me to steal the dead money immediately or take the betting initiative into the post-flop with a hand that can flop well.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 8h. The Small Blind has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (200 to win 600 total) and the fact that I will have position on the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand, J8o is a standard defending hand in this spot. I will call to see a flop.

With a Queen-high flush draw on a 3h 5s Jh flop, I have strong equity and fold equity. Betting continues the aggression from pre-flop, allowing me to take down the pot immediately or build it for when I hit my flush. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard and puts pressure on the Big Blind's marginal hands.

I have top pair with an eight kicker on a board of Jh 5s 3h. Pro's lead of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. My hand is too strong to fold at this point, but raising would likely only get action from better hands (AJ, KJ, QJ, or sets) and fold out his bluffs. By calling, I keep his range wide and can re-evaluate on the turn with the advantage of position.

The King of Clubs on the turn is an excellent card for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser. Although I only have a Queen-high flush draw, double-barreling puts significant pressure on Flash's marginal made hands like a weak Jack, middle pair, or smaller draws. A bet of 800 (two-thirds pot) maximizes my fold equity while building the pot for the times I complete my flush on the river.

The King of Clubs on the turn is a very unfavorable card for my middle pair. Pro's sizing of 800 into a 1200 pot represents significant strength, likely a King (AK, KQ, KJ) or a superior Jack (AJ, QJ). My J8 has very few outs to improve if I am behind, and calling this bet would likely lead to facing another large bet on the river. Given the tournament context and the strength shown by the opponent, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

We are on the button with 23 suited. While we have position, this is the weakest suited connector and has very low expected value. Raising as a steal is an option, but with 150 hands in the tournament and 112bb deep, we don't need to force the action with bottom-tier hands. We can wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce suited (Ah 2h). Since the Button (Pro) has folded, I am in a Blind vs Blind situation against Flashlight. Ah 2h is a strong hand in this spot, possessing significant equity and post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and build the pot with a hand that is likely ahead of the Big Blind's random range.

4c 8s is a very weak, uncoordinated hand that does not play well post-flop. Calling a raise out of position with this hand is negative expected value. It is more disciplined to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with K6o in a 3-handed game. While not a premium hand, K6o is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to maintain aggression and leverage my positional advantage.

With Ace-Nine offsuit in the small blind, I am facing a raise from the button. Folding is too conservative given the pot odds and my relative position, but three-betting OOP into a button raiser can bloat the pot and put me in a difficult spot. Calling is a balanced approach that defends my blind, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to see a flop with a decent high-card holding. I will call the 250 to complete the 300 total.

Getting 4.5 to 1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 700), King-Nine suited is a standard and profitable defend from the big blind. The hand plays well post-flop multi-way, as it can flop strong top pairs, flushes, or straight draws.

I am in the small blind out of position against two opponents on a board of 4c Js Jc. With Ace-high, I have very little equity to bet for value and my hand is not strong enough to bluff into two players on this paired board. The best strategy is to check and assess the action from my opponents. If there is heavy betting, I can easily fold, but I want to keep the pot small or get a free card if possible.

With King-Nine of hearts on a 4c Js Jc flop, I completely missed the board, having only King-high with no draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser and facing multiple opponents, checking is the standard and safest play. I have no equity to justify a bet and will likely fold to any subsequent aggression.

I was the pre-flop aggressor. The flop comes J-J-4, which is quite dry but dangerous if someone has a Jack. Both Flashlight and Pro checked. While a continuation bet could represent a Jack or an overpair, checking back with King-high allows me to see a turn for free and keeps the pot manageable. My hand has some showdown value against complete bluffs, and I do not want to get check-raised off my equity. I will check and evaluate the turn.

The turn card (3s) is a relative blank that does not help my Ace-high hand improve. Betting into two opponents on this paired board would require a significant bluff, which is inefficient with little fold equity. Checking allows me to control the pot size and preserve my stack while keeping the option to fold if facing aggressive action. Check-calling or check-folding is the optimal path to showdown or safely exiting the hand.

With King-Nine of hearts on a 4c Js Jc 3s board, I still only have King high and absolutely no draws. The turn brought a second flush draw, making the board wetter, and I have zero equity to continue aggressively. Checking into my opponents is the safest play, as I have no value to bet and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against two players who might have picked up draws or are holding small pairs.

The board remains paired with Jacks and the 3s on the turn is largely a blank. Both opponents have checked twice, suggesting they are either slow-playing a Jack or, more likely, have marginal hands or air. My King-high has some showdown value and I can still improve on the river. By checking behind again, I control the size of the pot and get to see the river for free without risking a check-raise.

With Ace-high and no pair, my hand is strictly a bluff-catcher that has little showdown value against any pair. Since the action has been checked around on both the flop and turn, betting would turn my hand into a bluff, which is high-risk against two opponents. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown cheaply, as any bet would likely only be called by better hands or induce a fold from worse hands that might accidentally win at showdown.

On the river, I still only have King high. Since the flop and turn checked through, my opponents likely have weak ranges, possibly missed draws, low pocket pairs, or Ace high. King high actually retains some marginal showdown value against busted draws like Queen-high or worse. Bluffing here is risky, as it's hard to represent a strong hand after checking the flop and turn, and I'd only get called by better hands like Ace-high or a small pair. Checking to see a free showdown is the most logical choice.

The board has paired Jacks and very little else happened throughout the hand. Both Flashlight and Pro have checked on the flop, turn, and river, indicating significant weakness or very marginal holdings. While my King-high (Kc 6s) is not a strong hand, it has reasonable showdown value in a pot that has been checked down entirely. Betting here might not fold out small pairs that are suspicious of a late bluff, and checking allows me to realize my equity and see the showdown for free. I will check back.

Qc-2c is a marginal hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. While stealing the blinds is important in short-handed play, Q2s has poor equity against most calling ranges. Given the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger hand to initiate aggression.

K5o is a marginal hand out of position in a blind versus blind situation. Against an aggressive opponent like Flash, the best and lowest-variance play is to simply fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am on the button with T3o, which is a very weak offsuit hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand falls way outside any acceptable opening range. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit. The action folded to me. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, T3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to conserve my stack.

In a three-handed game, opening from the button with suited hands like 9s 5s is standard to put pressure on the blinds and utilize positional advantage. I have consistently used a 3x opening size (300 chips) in previous hands, so I will maintain that sizing here to remain balanced and difficult to read.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a strong starting hand that plays best when isolating the opponent to keep the pot heads-up, especially since I am out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 takes the initiative away from the button, forces the big blind to likely fold, and sets up a continuation bet scenario if needed. This is a standard aggressive play with this range.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 6h, a very weak suited hand. I'm facing an initial open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This is a clear fold due to terrible equity against a 3-betting range and being out of position.

I opened 9s 5s from the button, which is a standard loose-aggressive play in 3-handed games to steal the blinds. However, Flashlight's 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind shows significant strength. While I have the advantage of being in position, 9s 5s is too weak to call a large 3-bet. It lacks the connectivity and high-card value necessary to realize its equity effectively post-flop. Folding here preserves my stack for better spots where I have more equity or blockers.

I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Four of Clubs, which is a very weak, disconnected hand with no potential. Even on the button, this hand does not have enough equity to justify calling a blind or raising. Playing weak hands out of position or even in position is a quick way to bleed chips, so folding is the disciplined, correct strategic choice.
KQs is a premium hand, especially blind versus blind. Raising to 300 is a standard play to build the pot for value, gain initiative, and put pressure on the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 96o. Pro raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, 96o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. It is better to fold now and wait for a more advantageous situation than to risk chips with a sub-optimal hand.

J6 offsuit is a very weak hand, even from the Button. With two players left to act behind me who could wake up with a better hand or 3-bet, it is best to simply fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

With the Button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation. A9o is a very strong hand in this scenario, significantly ahead of the average range the Big Blind will be defending. Raising to 300 is a standard move to either take the blinds immediately or build a pot where I have a range advantage, despite being out of position for the rest of the hand.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. I hold suited cards (Jack-8 of spades), which have decent potential to hit a flush or straight. Given the pot odds and the position, calling to see a flop is the most standard and profitable approach. I will call the additional 200 chips to complete the 300 chip bet.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board of 9-7-3 with two hearts. In a blind-vs-blind situation, this is an extremely strong hand. I need to bet for value and to charge potential draws (flush draws or straight draws like 8-10 or 6-8). A continuation bet of 300, which is 50% of the pot, is a standard and effective play here.

I am facing a bet of 300 into a pot of 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. With a double gutshot straight draw (needing a 6 or 10 to complete a straight) and a backdoor flush draw, I have sufficient equity to justify a call. Folding would be too conservative given the board texture and my draw, while raising here would be turning my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I still have potential to improve significantly on the turn.

The Queen of Diamonds on the turn is a significant overcard that could have hit the Big Blind's defending range (hands like QJ, QT, or even Q7s). While I still have second pair with a top kicker, my hand's relative strength has decreased. Betting again risks bloating the pot against a range that now contains more hands that beat me. By checking, I can control the size of the pot, see how Flashlight reacts to the overcard, and potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or get to a cheaper showdown.

The opponent has checked on the turn, presenting an opportunity to see a free river card. My hand (Jack-high) currently has very little showdown value, but I have a gutshot straight draw needing a Ten. Since the opponent showed weakness by checking, betting might be an option, but checking behind is the most disciplined approach to control the pot size while attempting to realize my equity for free.

On the river, the board pairs the seven. I currently hold two pair, Nines and Sevens, with an Ace kicker. The turn went check-check, suggesting Flashlight likely doesn't have a strong Queen or was playing a draw/marginal hand passively. By checking the river, I control the pot size and allow Flashlight to bluff with missed draws (like hearts or various straight draws) that would have folded to a bet. I have a strong enough hand to call most reasonable bets, and checking ensures I don't value-bet myself against a slow-played seven or a Queen.

I am in the big blind with Jack-high, which has virtually no showdown value on this board (9h 7h 3d Qd 7c). My opponent, Flash, has checked the river, indicating they are likely giving up on the hand or have a weak hand that does not want to bet. While I could attempt a bluff, the board texture is wet enough that Flash is likely to call with any pair (any 3, 7, 9, or Queen). Since I cannot beat any pair, checking behind is the most disciplined and safe option to avoid inflating the pot with a losing hand and to minimize any further losses.

In a three-handed game, the Button should open a wide range of hands to put pressure on the blinds. 98o is a standard opening hand in this position. I have been consistently raising to 300 in previous hands when entering the pot, so I will maintain that sizing to keep my range balanced and take advantage of my positional advantage.

Q7 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against an open raise from the button. Being out of position against the button makes it difficult to realize equity with such a marginal holding. It is the most disciplined and profitable play to fold here and wait for a better opportunity to engage.
96 offsuit is a very weak hand, even against a button open. Playing this hand out of position is highly disadvantageous and negative EV. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

5-3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand that lacks playability and strength, making it unsuitable for entering the pot from the button. Folding is the most profitable and disciplined decision in this spot to preserve my stack.

AhTh is a strong suited broadway hand. When folded to me in the Small Blind, raising is the best play to steal the blinds or build the pot with a strong holding against the Big Blind's wide range. I will raise to 300 chips (3x BB).

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 7h. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position post-flop against the Small Blind, 97o is a weak hand that does not play well against a 3x open. Given the early stage of the tournament and my stack size, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for better opportunities rather than calling and potentially facing difficult decisions on later streets.
J9o is a marginal, unsuited hand that doesn't play very well post-flop. As the first to act on the Button, folding is the standard and safest play to conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 7s. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. 87o is a speculative hand with some connectivity. It is only 50 more to call to see a flop, which provides good pot odds (50 to win 150). I will complete the bet to see a flop and re-evaluate, rather than folding or making a risky raise against an active Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 6-2 offsuit. Since the Small Blind has completed to match the big blind, the action is on me. Checking is the standard play here as it allows me to see a flop for free without committing further chips with a dominated holding.

I am in a blind-on-blind battle. The flop is Ts 5h Qc, giving me no pair and no direct draw besides a backdoor straight draw. The pot is small (200), and Flashlight has checked. Since I was the one who completed from the small blind, a check here is the most standard play to see if I can catch a piece of the turn for free. There is no reason to bluff into two broadway cards that likely hit a big blind's range or high cards.

I hold 6s-2s on a Ts-5h-Qc board. With only a backdoor flush draw and no pair or credible straight potential, my hand has very little equity. Since the pot is small (200) and my opponent checked, the most rational play is to check behind to see a free card. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against a range that could easily contain a Ten, a Queen, or a middle pair.

The turn pairs the Ten on a board of Ts 5h Qc Th. I have 8-high with no pair or draw. Since the flop was checked by both players, I have no reason to believe I have the best hand, nor do I have enough equity to warrant a semi-bluff. I will check and hope to see a free river card, or fold if Flashlight decides to bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s-2s. The board is Ts-5h-Qc-Th. I have no made hand, no draw, and no showdown value. Checking is the only optimal play here as betting would be an unnecessary bluff against a range that checked the flop and turn, offering no potential to improve the pot equity, while checking allows me to see the river for free.

The river completes a very coordinated board (Ts 5h Qc Th Ah). I have 8-high and have shown no strength throughout the hand. The pot is very small (200 chips), and there is little reason to attempt a bluff on a board that contains an Ace, Queen, and Ten, as these cards are likely to have connected with the Big Blind's range or at least provided them with a better high card. I will check and concede the pot unless my opponent also has nothing lower than 8-high.

I have 6-high, which has zero showdown value on a board of Ts-5h-Qc-Th-Ah. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a range that could easily contain an Ace, a Queen, a Ten, or even a pair of Fives, all of which beat me. Since my opponent checked, checking behind is the only rational play to conclude the hand without committing further chips to a pot I cannot win.

I am on the button with Td 9c, which is a solid hand to open in a three-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to exert pressure on the blinds. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size in previous hands, and I will maintain that sizing to remain balanced and take control of the pot early.

My hand, 7-3 offsuit, is extremely weak and holds no significant equity. As the small blind, I am OOP against the button and still have the big blind to act behind me. Calling a raise with such a hand is a negative expected value (-EV) play. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

My hand is Deuce-Six suited (2h 6h). Facing a 3x open raise from the Button while I'm in the Big Blind. This hand is far too weak to defend, even with suitedness, because it lacks high card value, connectedness, and playability out of position. The correct play is to fold.

7-8 offsuit is a marginal holding, but in a 3-handed game on the button, it is a reasonable hand to open-raise to 300 to attempt to steal the blinds and take the initiative, especially given my recent passive play.
Ten-Eight offsuit is a weak holding, especially when facing a raise from the Button while out of position in the Small Blind. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 2h, which is arguably the weakest starting hand in poker. Flashlight raised from the button to 300. While I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, 32o performs poorly post-flop and I would be out of position. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand in this spot.

Jack-Three suited is a very weak hand, even on the button in a 3-handed game. Opening here would be too loose and risks unnecessarily bloating the pot with a marginal holding against the blinds. Folding is the standard, conservative play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 3c. The Button (Pro) has folded. It is now up to me to decide whether to fold, call (complete the small blind), or raise against the Big Blind (Flashlight). 8c 3c is a very weak hand, even in a blind-vs-blind situation. While completing the blind costs only 50 more chips, the hand has poor playability and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given the tournament context and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with an Ace in a 3-handed game. Ace-Deuce offsuit is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of being last to act post-flop. I will maintain my consistent opening size of 300 to keep my range balanced.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in a blind versus button scenario. Since Flash has been active with button opens, a 3-bet is the optimal strategy to reclaim initiative, deny equity, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. Raising to 1000 effectively puts pressure on the opener while still being a manageable sizing given the current stack depths.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Three offsuit, a very weak hand. The Button opened to 300 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 1000. This indicates strong ranges from both opponents, making a fold the only viable option.

Flashlight's 3-bet to 1000 represents significant strength. While I have the button, A2o is easily dominated by better Aces in his 3-betting range and doesn't flop well enough to justify a call of 700 more. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

7d 6h is an unsuited, low-value hand with poor playability. Playing weak offsuit connectors in a 3-handed game on the button, even without a raise, is generally not a profitable long-term strategy. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for stronger starting hands.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind with King-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind, denying free equity to weaker hands and building a pot for value.

The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Since the Button folded, I am in the Big Blind and will have the advantage of acting last on all post-flop streets. I am holding 9s 7c, which is a marginal hand, but given the 2:1 pot odds and my positional advantage over the Small Blind, it is worth defending. I will call the 200 chips to see a flop.

As the pre-flop aggressor out of position, I completely missed this flop and hold no flush or backdoor draws. However, the board is low and relatively disconnected, meaning Flash likely missed as well unless he holds a pocket pair, an 8, or a heart draw. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) applies pressure, represents a stronger range (like an overpair or strong Broadway), and can fold out his high-card hands or random floats, allowing me to take down the pot uncontested.

On a flop of 8h-3h-2s, I am holding 9-high with no draws or significant overcards. Pro, who raised pre-flop, has made a standard half-pot continuation bet. While I have position, my hand has very little equity to continue and no real backdoor potential. Even though the pot odds are 3:1, calling with 9-high into a pre-flop aggressor is not a profitable long-term strategy here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With QTo on the Button, I have a playable hand that is strong enough to open-raise. By raising to 300 (3x the big blind), I can either steal the blinds right now or take the initiative into the flop in position.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The button (Pro) has raised to 3BB, and I am in the small blind, meaning I would be out of position for the rest of the hand if I called. Given the hand strength and positional disadvantage, this is an easy fold.

Pocket fours are a strong hand heads-up against a standard button open. With a call price of 200 into a 450 pot, the pot odds are favorable. While I am out of position, set-mining or playing a small ball strategy on low boards is a solid approach given the stack depth. Raising 3-bet out of position with 44 is higher variance and less desirable, so calling is the standard and most prudent play.

I am out of position with an underpair (pocket fours) on a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor (Queen and Jack high). My hand has very little equity against a continuing range that likely contains high cards or draws. Betting here would be a weak bluff, as I don't have enough fold equity against an opponent's opening range. Checking is the most prudent play to see if the turn offers any improvement or if I can reach showdown cheaply.

We flopped top pair with a ten kicker on a highly coordinated board (Qs 5s Jc). Betting here is essential for protection against various straight and flush draws, and it allows us to extract value from weaker pairs like Jx or flush-draws. A bet of roughly half the pot helps define our opponent's range without overcommitting our stack if we happen to be outkicked.

The board is Qs 5s Jc, which is a wet, coordinated board hitting the opponent's (Pro) opening range significantly better than my own. My pocket fours are an underpair with no meaningful draw and limited potential to improve to the best hand. Calling a 350 bet into a 1000 pot requires roughly 26% equity, which I likely do not have against a standard continuation betting range. Continuing out of position with such a weak holding would be a mistake, as I would likely be forced to fold to further aggression on the turn or river anyway. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

I am on the button with Q7o. While opening ranges are wider in 3-handed play, Q7o is at the bottom of the range and vulnerable to the frequent 3-betting I've seen from Flashlight in the small blind. Given my current stack size and the aggression at the table, I will fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opening opportunity.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, a reasonably strong hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind after the button has folded. Since the pot currently contains only the blinds and I have the initiative to act, raising is the optimal play to pressure the big blind and potentially take down the pot immediately or see the flop with the betting lead.

I am dealt 63o in the big blind, which is a very weak and unconnected hand. The small blind has raised to 300. Calling with such a poor hand out of position (if considering future streets relative to the raiser, though against SB I'd have position, it's still a trash hand) or raising as a bluff is not profitable here. Folding is the optimal decision.

Seven-eight offsuit is a weak, unsuited hand with poor equity and limited playability. As I am the first to act pre-flop, there is no need to commit chips to the pot with such a marginal holding. It is more disciplined to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand that provides better post-flop opportunities.

With a small pocket pair (3h3c) folded to me in the Small Blind, I have a profitable open-raise opportunity. Raising to 3x (300) puts pressure on the Big Blind, aiming to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, K2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the Small Blind's opening range. Given Pro's aggressive history in previous hands, I expect to face significant pressure on most flops. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal hand.

I am holding J3o on the Button. This is a very weak hand with extremely limited playability and poor equity against any calling range. The standard and optimal play is to fold it pre-flop to avoid bleeding chips.

Betting into this board with nothing would be a purely speculative bluff that is unlikely to succeed against the Small Blind's raising range.

The turn brings the Nine of Hearts, completing a possible flush and adding more straight potential to an already coordinated board of Ah-Th-6s. My pocket deuces have almost no equity here, especially since I do not hold a significant heart blocker. After checking the flop and seeing my opponent check back, I have no reason to believe my hand is ahead or that a bluff would be effective against a range that likely includes many pairs or draws. I will check again, intending to fold to any bet, as the risk-reward ratio for defending this hand is extremely poor.

I have 4-high with no pair, no flush draw, and no open-ended straight draw. The board is [Ah, Th, 6s, 9h]. Betting here would be a pure bluff against an opponent who could easily have an Ace, a Ten, or a made flush draw given the heavy hearts. Checking behind allows me to see a free river card and keeps the pot small without risking more chips on a hand with zero showdown value.

On this river, my pocket deuces are the absolute bottom of my range and have almost no showdown value against any pair. However, the board is quite scary with an Ace and three hearts present. My opponent, Flashlight, has checked twice, suggesting they also likely have a weak hand or a missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff to push off a weak pair like a 6 or a 5, the pot is small (600) and it is not worth risking a significant portion of my stack or invite a potential bluff-catch from a stubborn opponent. I will check and hope to either win a very unlikely showdown against a lower high-card or simply concede the pot if they have any piece of the board.

I have rivered a pair of fives on a board of [Ah, Th, 6s, 9h, 5s]. My hand is very weak in the context of the hand's action, where my opponent (the pre-flop aggressor) has checked three times. Checking behind is the most prudent decision, as betting would likely only be called by better hands (like an Ace or Ten) and folding is not an option when I have reached the river with showdown value. Checking allows me to take the showdown without risking further chips on a weak hand.

In this 3-handed game, I am on the button with Queen-Six offsuit. While wider opening ranges are standard on the button, Q6o is near the bottom of the range and susceptible to 3-bets from Flashlight, who has shown a tendency to 3-bet aggressively from the blinds (as seen in hands #7, #16, and #24). With Pro also holding a large stack in the Big Blind, I will choose to fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications out of position or facing a 3-bet.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (9d 3c). The button (Flash) has folded. It is not strategically sound to invest more chips into the pot with such a poor holding against the big blind (Pro). Therefore, I will fold to minimize losses.

I am holding 7s 6d, which is a weak hand with low equity. Given that I have the smallest stack at the table, I should be disciplined and only play hands with higher potential. Folding preserves my stack of 4,650 chips, allowing me to wait for a more favorable situation to enter a pot.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, 8c 5s offsuit. The Button has folded. Against the Big Blind, this hand is not worth investing any more chips to see a flop. Folding is the correct choice here.

I am on the button with 98o, a marginal offsuit connector. While a steal attempt could sometimes work, it's generally unprofitable to open such a weak hand, especially with the Big Blind having a slightly shorter stack that might induce a wider 3-bet jam. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. 7-6 offsuit is a speculative hand but in 3-handed play, it's strong enough to attempt a blind steal, especially since Flashlight has been folding frequently to pre-flop aggression. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the shorter stack and try to win the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a heads-up pot against the Small Blind. Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, and I have positional advantage post-flop. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand, define my range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a profitable continuation bet if called. A raise to 900 is a standard 3-bet sizing to exert pressure.

I attempted a blind steal with 7-6 offsuit, but Flashlight responded with a 3-bet. Looking at previous hands, Flashlight has shown significant strength when 3-betting (Hands #7, #16, #24), and I have consistently folded to this aggression. 7-6 offsuit is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, especially since a call would commit a significant portion of the pot relative to Flashlight's remaining stack. Folding is the best way to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 7d 4s. This is a very weak hand. While opening from the button is a standard aggressive play in three-handed poker, 74o is near the bottom of the range and generally not worth the risk of a 3-bet from the blinds or playing out of position/post-flop with such poor equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a marginal hand (Queen-Six offsuit) after the button has folded. Since it is heads-up against the big blind, I will complete the bet to see a cheap flop rather than folding, as I have already committed 50 chips to the pot.

With 84 suited in the big blind against a small blind limp, I have a weak hand but a free opportunity to see the flop. Checking is the standard play here to realize equity without putting more chips at risk.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen. The board is relatively dry, and since I am heads-up against the Big Blind who checked their option pre-flop, they are unlikely to have a strong hand. A small value bet is appropriate to extract value from weaker Queens or other low pairs, and to define my hand strength.

On a board of 3c Qh 2h, my 8c 4c has absolutely no piece of the board and only very weak backdoor draws. The small blind is betting half the pot, making this an easy fold as I have virtually no equity.

I am holding 6-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor connectivity and no significant equity. With 50BB remaining, I need to be more selective with my hands. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic play in this position to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Ah7h is a strong suited ace in a blind vs blind scenario after the Button folds. A standard raise to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take the initiative with a hand that has good equity and playability post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. This is a standard blind vs. blind or late position open. My hand, Ace-Four, is strong enough to defend against a 3x open, especially with a stack of 120 BBs. Folding would be too tight. I have the option to 3-bet or call. Given Pro's active style in previous hands, I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable with a marginal Ace, rather than 3-betting and potentially facing a 4-bet shove from a similar stack.

I flopped top two pair on a very dry, rainbow board. This is a very strong hand, and I want to start building the pot immediately to extract value from any weaker Ax hands or pocket pairs in the Big Blind's range. A half-pot bet of 300 serves as a standard continuation bet that can easily get called by worse hands.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, albeit with a weak kicker (4). The board is extremely dry (3-A-7 rainbow), which is generally a good board for the pre-flop aggressor to continuation bet. Pro has bet 300 into a 600 pot. Folding top pair here would be far too exploitable. Raising is not ideal because I would likely only get action from better Aces or sets, effectively turning my hand into a bluff or value-owning myself. Calling is the best option to keep the pot manageable, keep Pro's bluffs in his range, and see how the turn develops.

With top two pair on a board that now features straight and flush draws, I need to continue betting for value and protection. A 2/3 pot bet will extract value from weaker Ax hands and charge any draws a premium to see the river.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Fours) on the turn. The board is 3-A-7-4 rainbow (with a backdoor spade draw). My opponent, Pro, has led out for 800 into a 1200 pot, showing continued aggression. While my hand is very strong, raising here might force out his bluffs or weaker Aces (like A8, A9, AT) that I want to keep in the pot. By calling, I maintain the disguise of a marginal Ace (which is how I played the flop) and allow Pro to potentially fire a third barrel on the river with a worse hand or a bluff. I only fear specific hands like A7, A3, 77, or 33, but given the action, I am ahead of the vast majority of his range. I will call to maximize value on the river.

The river 5h puts a very connected sequence on the board (3-4-5-7) and completes straights for any 6 or 2. Hands like A6, A2, 76, or even 65 are entirely consistent with the Big Blind's line of calling the flop and turn. While I have top two pair, betting here is risky as it might isolate me against a straight, and weaker Ax hands might fold anyway. Checking allows for pot control and gives my opponent the opportunity to bluff missed draws or value bet worse two pair.

I have two pair, Aces and Fours. The river card (5h) is potentially problematic as any 2 or 6 completes a straight, and hands like A5 or A7 now beat me. Pro has led for two streets and decided to check the river. This check often indicates either a desire to reach a cheap showdown with a marginal Ace (like A8-AJ) or a trap with a very strong hand. Given the board texture and the fact that I have significant showdown value, I will check behind. Betting might fold out his bluffs and only get called or raised by hands that have me beaten, so checking ensures I win the pot against his weaker range without risking a check-raise.

Pocket eights is a strong starting hand 3-handed. As the first to act on the Button, I want to open with my standard raise size of 3 big blinds to build the pot for value, protect my equity, and potentially take down the blinds uncontested.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a frequent play for him. In previous hands (#39, #51, #72), I have successfully 3-bet Pro's button opens with Ace-high hands, and he folded each time. While my hand is relatively weak if called, a 3-bet to around 3x-3.5x his raise often takes down the pot immediately, which is a better outcome than playing a weak Ace out of position. I will raise to 950.

J6o is a very weak hand with no playability, especially when facing an open raise from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind. Defending the big blind here would be a significant mistake as the hand has poor equity against the likely ranges involved in this 3-bet pot. Folding is the standard, disciplined play.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind, pocket eights are too strong to fold but not quite strong enough to comfortably 4-bet for value. Calling allows me to play in position, realize my equity, and see a flop. If I hit a set, the implied odds against a 3-bettor's strong range are excellent.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet. The flop is 9h-6s-Ts, which is quite coordinated and likely hits a significant portion of Pro's calling range (like mid-pairs or suited connectors). However, as the 3-better, I still represent a very strong range (JJ+). In previous hands where Pro was the 3-better (Hands #43 and #49), he checked and folded to a bet when the flop didn't favor his range. Since I have the lead, I will put out a continuation bet of about 35% of the pot (700 chips). This allows me to take the pot down if Pro has missed with hands like KQ or AJ, while keeping the price relatively cheap if he has hit the board and decides to continue.

On a very wet flop of 9h 6s Ts, my pocket eights have been counterfeited by two overcards. I only have a marginal gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and two outs for a set. Given that Flash 3-bet pre-flop and is now continuing on a board that heavily favors their range (overpairs, big broadways, and strong flush draws), calling here is unprofitable as many turn cards will be difficult to play and my hand is mostly a bluff-catcher. Folding is the safest and most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 11,800 chips, and I want to maintain my aggressive image while playing in position. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) has been my standard opening size and allows me to either win the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage and positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with a weak, uncoordinated hand [6h, 4d]. Calling out of position against a button raise is negative expected value, and there is no reason to inflate the pot or attempt a bluff with this specific holding. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 54s, a suited connector that plays well deep-stacked. Facing a standard 3x open from the Button, I have good pot odds and implied odds to see a flop. The effective stack is over 100 BBs, giving me plenty of room to maneuver post-flop if I hit a draw or a strong hand.

I flopped bottom pair and a flush draw on an Ace-high board. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most balanced play is to check to the aggressor. If he continuation bets, my hand has plenty of equity to continue, and checking allows him to bluff or value bet thinner hands.

On the button, I raised pre-flop with Ace-Five offsuit and the Big Blind (Pro) called. The flop comes Ace-High with two hearts (Ah-7h-4s), giving me top pair. Pro checks to me. My range as the pre-flop raiser heavily favors this board. I have plenty of Aces in my range (AJ, AQ, AK, A7s, A4s) as well as some flush draws. Betting here achieves two things: it gets value from weaker Aces (though my kicker is weak), draws (especially hearts), and middle pairs (88-KK), and it protects my hand against overcards or potential flush draws. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot is appropriate to maintain pressure and build the pot.

Facing a continuation bet on an Ace-high board, I have bottom pair and a flush draw. This gives me excellent equity even against top pair. Calling is the standard and most profitable line here, as it keeps my opponent's bluffs in the pot, controls the pot size out of position, and gives me a great price to see the turn.

On the turn, the 9c doesn't improve my bottom pair, but I still have a strong flush draw. Out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. If Flash bets, I can evaluate the sizing and likely continue with my equity; if he checks back, I get a free river card to try and hit my flush, two pair, or trips.

On the turn, the 9c is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, although it could theoretically complete a straight for a hand like 86 or 56, which are unlikely. Pro has checked to me again after calling my flop bet. While I have top pair, my kicker (5) is very weak. If Pro is also holding an Ace, I am likely out-kicked by hands like A8, AT, or AJ. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, protect myself against a potential check-raise, and keep the pot manageable for a showdown. It also allows me to see the river for free and potentially induce a bluff from missed draws on the final street.

On the river, I have bottom pair on an A-K high board and my flush draw missed. My pair of 4s has some marginal showdown value against missed straight draws or random high-card floats, but it is too weak to value bet. The river King hits a large portion of my opponent's turn checking range, such as KQ, KJ, or KT, making a bluff very risky and unlikely to succeed. Checking allows me to potentially win at showdown against total air, while controlling the pot size and preparing to fold if he bets.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board that has remained relatively dry despite the heart draw. My opponent has checked for the third time (flop call, turn check, river check). While I have showdown value, betting here is risky because most hands that would call me (like A8 or better) have me out-kicked, and hands I beat (like 7x or missed heart draws) are unlikely to call a bet. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot against those weaker hands or missed draws without the risk of being check-raised.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine offsuit, which is a playable hand for late position to try and steal the blinds. My stack is shorter than my opponents, so taking control of the pot pre-flop is a solid strategy. Raising to 300 is consistent with my typical opening size.
J3 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise, the standard and mathematically sound play is to fold from the Small Blind, avoiding playing a trash hand out of position.

I have a premium hand (AKo) in the Big Blind against a button raise. This is a clear 3-bet for value and to take control of the hand out of position. I opened to 300 on the button with Q9o, a standard steal attempt, but Flash (the Big Blind) has 3-bet to 950. With 98o on the button, the hand is too weak to profitably open-raise, even in a 3-handed game. It lacks suitedness, making it less playable post-flop, and opening would expose me to 3-bets from the blinds. Folding is the most standard and solid choice.

I have QJs in the Small Blind. This is a very strong drawing hand and a premium suited connector. With the Button (Pro) folding, the action is on me. Flashlight is in the Big Blind with a relatively short stack of 4,500 compared to my 12,750. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit, facing a raise from the small blind. The pot odds justify a call here to defend my blind. I do not have a strong enough hand to re-raise, but folding would be too passive given the position. I will call the 200 additional chips to see the flop.

On a monotone flop of hearts (2h 3h Kh), I have two overcards (Qs Js) but no hearts. As the pre-flop aggressor, I could C-bet to represent a King or a high heart, but this board is quite dangerous. My hand has very little equity if called or raised, so keeping the pot small is the most prudent play.

The board is highly coordinated with two hearts and connected cards, making a pair of deuces extremely vulnerable. Betting into a checked opponent on this texture often bloats the pot with a weak hand, while checking allows for pot control and the chance to see a free turn card. Given my stack size, playing cautiously to minimize risk while assessing the turn is the most prudent strategy.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds (Jd) has arrived, giving me second pair with a Queen kicker. While this improves my hand, the flop was monotone hearts (2h 3h Kh), which is still very threatening. By checking again, I am exercising pot control and looking to reach showdown with my modest pair. If Flashlight checks again, I can potentially value bet or check-call the river depending on the card.

I am in position with a very weak pair of deuces on a dangerous board featuring three hearts and connecting high cards. Betting into the Small Blind here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and will likely only isolate me against a heart draw or a better pair. On the river, the Queen of Clubs (Qc) has improved my hand to two pair, Queens and Jacks. I will place a value bet of half the pot (300) to get paid by those weaker hands. If I am raised, I will have to carefully reconsider, as a raise here would likely indicate a straight or a flush.

The board is [2h, 3h, Kh, Jd, Qc]. My pair of deuces is extremely weak on a board with multiple overcards (K, Q, J). Given the high density of cards that beat my deuces, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher that loses to almost all value-betting ranges. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I'm on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. This sizing is consistent with my previous pre-flop opening raises throughout the session.

Holding 6s 8h out of position against a button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity and playing out of position will lead to difficult decisions and likely negative expected value. Preserving the stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.

I am in the big blind facing a button raise with 74o, which is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. It's a standard fold as it has very poor playability and equity against a button opening range.

6h 7d is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low equity, especially in a multi-way pot. With my short stack relative to the other players, playing speculative hands out of position or in marginal spots is not optimal. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four suited. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see a flop, and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Out of position against the big blind, the board of 2h Th 8d connects heavily with their pre-flop calling range, which includes flush draws, straight draws, and various pairs. Holding only seven-high with weak backdoor possibilities, a continuation bet here is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable. Checking to evaluate the action is the safest play.

I have top pair (tens) on a board of 10-8-2. Although my kicker is weak (4), checking back gives the opponent a free card to potentially hit an overcard or complete a draw. Since the Small Blind (Pro) checked to me, I will bet 300 (half the pot) to take the initiative, protect my hand, and extract value from worse hands like 8x or various draws.

I raised pre-flop with 7s 5s and was called by the Big Blind. The flop of 2h Th 8d missed my hand completely, leaving me with seven-high and very weak backdoor possibilities. Since I checked and my opponent bet, I have no equity to justify continuing out of position. Folding is the only viable option.

With A9o on the Button, I have a solid hand to open-raise and attempt to steal the blinds. Being in position gives me an advantage post-flop. A standard open to 3x the big blind (300) is a profitable play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

My hand, 4s-2s, is extremely weak and offers very little potential for improvement. I am on the button with T9o, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at 135 BBs, while Flashlight is relatively short at 41 BBs and Pro is close to me in chips. I am in the small blind with Ace-Seven offsuit, which is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position against an aggressive button raiser. Given my significantly smaller stack compared to my opponents, preserving my chips for a stronger opportunity is the most disciplined strategic play. Folding avoids playing a bloated pot from an unfavorable position.
K5s is a reasonable hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. We are getting good odds and our hand has suited and high card potential to flop top pair or a flush draw.

I defended the Big Blind and flopped a strong King-high flush draw. The standard and most balanced play out of position is to check to the pre-flop raiser. Depending on whether Flash bets and the sizing he uses, I can decide to check-call to realize my equity or check-raise as a semi-bluff to put maximum pressure on him.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of 7d-9d-2s. The board is somewhat coordinated with two diamonds and potential straight draws. Pro has checked to me, which is standard for the pre-flop caller. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot (300) to extract value from weaker hands like 7x, pocket pairs lower than 9s, and various draws (diamond flushes or straight draws like 8-10 or 6-8), while also protecting my hand from overcards on the turn.

With a King-high flush draw, we have a strong hand with plenty of equity. Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser, we have the direct odds to call. While a check-raise semi-bluff is an option, check-calling allows us to control the size of the pot out of position, keep all of our opponent's bluffs in his range, and realize our equity safely on the turn.

The Ace on the turn heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Checking to the raiser is the standard and most balanced play out of position. I still hold a strong King-high flush draw, and checking allows me to evaluate my opponent's action. A donk bet here would be risky against an Ace.

The turn brings an Ace of Clubs, which is a significant scare card. While the Ace is generally better for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Pro's check-call on the flop (7d-9d-2s) indicates he has a piece of the board or a draw. If I bet, I risk being check-raised by an Ace or a slow-played hand, and I don't want to turn a hand with showdown value into a bluff. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially catch bluffs or missed draws from Pro on the final street.

We missed our flush draw and our King high has virtually no showdown value on this board. Flash's decision to check behind on the turn when the Ace fell strongly suggests he does not have a strong Ace and is likely holding a marginal pair (like 88-QQ, 9x) or missed high cards that gave up. By betting around two-thirds of the pot on the river, we can credibly represent an Ace or a strong value hand to fold out his bluff-catchers and win the pot uncontested.

On the river, the board is 7d-9d-2s-Ac-4h and the pot is 1250. Pro leads for 800. My hand is second pair (9s) with a Ten kicker. I checked the turn (the Ace) for pot control and to realize my equity, which likely signaled to Pro that I do not have an Ace. I am in the Button position with a weak starting hand, Jack-Six offsuit. Given my short stack size relative to the opponents, playing speculative or marginal hands is suboptimal. I will fold to preserve my stack.

With King-Eight suited in the Small Blind and the Button folding, this is a prime opportunity to raise. K8s is a strong starting hand in blind-versus-blind scenarios. A standard 3x raise to 300 will often win the pot immediately and if called, I have a very playable hand post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s6d, which is a weak hand. Pro in the Small Blind raised to 300, requiring me to call 200 more into a pot of 400. I am holding an extremely weak and disconnected offsuit hand (3h 8d). Even from the button, there is no value in playing this hand. I will simply fold and wait for a better starting hand to put my chips in.

I am in the small blind with 9-4 offsuit. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the big blind (Flashlight). 9-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with very poor equity and playability post-flop. Even in a blind-versus-blind scenario, completing the bet or raising with such a weak hand is generally unprofitable. Given that Flashlight has enough chips to defend and I would be out of position, the most disciplined play is to fold.

This is a standard play in blind-versus-blind scenarios to capitalize on position, even with a marginal hand, rather than folding and conceding the small blind immediately.

With a medium pocket pair (6h 6s) against a Small Blind limp, I want to take the initiative. Raising from the Big Blind puts pressure on a likely weak limping range, allows me to take control of the hand, and gives me a good chance to either win the pot immediately or play a larger pot in position.

Holding Queen-Five offsuit in the small blind against a raise from the big blind is a marginal situation. While I initially completed the small blind, the subsequent raise to 300 forces me to reconsider. Calling here would put me out of position with a weak hand that is easily dominated by the big blind's opening range. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent strategic choice to preserve my stack rather than committing more chips to a potentially losing situation post-flop.

I am holding 9-3 offsuit, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With no raise in front of me, I could technically call the 100 to see a flop, but there is no strategic value in playing such a poor hand out of position post-flop or even in position. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play long-term to preserve my stack.

Jack-Three suited is a very weak hand, even in a blind-vs-blind situation. Being out of position against the Big Blind makes it difficult to realize equity or successfully bluff post-flop. The best decision is to fold and save my chips for a better starting hand.
On the button with Jack-Nine suited, this is a standard open. The hand has good playability post-flop, and raising to 3 big blinds puts pressure on the blinds to fold or play out of position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s5c. Pro (Button) has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to be playing out of position against a strong opponent, especially since there is still another player (Flashlight) to act in the Big Blind. I am in the Big Blind with suited connectors (7s 5s). The raise from the Button is 300, and I only need to call 200 to see a flop. The pot odds are favorable (3.5:1), and suited connectors have good playability. Calling is the standard defense here given the stack depth and the price.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5s on a board of Td Kd 2c. I have completely missed this board with no pair and no real draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro on the button), checking is the standard and correct play to control the pot size while I re-evaluate on future streets.

I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts and a backdoor flush draw. The board hits my pre-flop raising range well (lots of Kings, Tens, and broadway cards). A continuation bet of around half the pot is a standard play to fold out the opponent's weaker holdings like Ace-high or underpairs, and it starts building the pot in case I hit my draw.

The flop is Td Kd 2c, and I hold 7s 5s. This board is extremely dry and hits the pre-flop aggressor's range (Button) very well, as it contains high cards (King and Ten). I have zero equity with 7-high, no draws, and no pair. Calling here would be a waste of chips given that I am out of position and have no way to improve to a winning hand unless I spike a runner-runner miracle, which is statistically non-viable. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with K8o. In a 3-handed game, King-high is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I will use my standard opening size of 300 chips.

I am in the small blind with T9 offsuit, which is a marginal hand to play out of position against a button raiser. Given the stack depths and the fact that I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and prudent course of action.

Facing a 3x button open with Jack-Seven suited in the Big Blind. While the hand has some suited potential, J7s is generally too weak to defend out of position against a solid opening range, especially for a 3x sizing. Folding here avoids marginal, tough spots post-flop.

Ace-seven offsuit is a strong enough holding in the button position to attempt a steal against the blinds. With my current stack size, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips. Raising to 300 (3BB) is a standard opening size in this spot to apply pressure and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop or gain initiative in position.

JTo is generally too weak to play from the small blind against a standard button open. We will be out of position post-flop, and our hand is easily dominated by better broadway combinations that the button might be raising for value. The best decision here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey suited. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, which usually represents a wide range of hands in a three-handed game. I am getting favorable pot odds to call, needing to commit 200 more chips into a total pot of 450 (offering roughly 2.25:1). Suited Queens have enough playability to defend in this spot, especially since the Small Blind has already folded, leaving me heads-up against the aggressor.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen on a relatively dry board of Q-8-4. Although my kicker is weak (Trey), in a heads-up pot against the Button's opening range, top pair is a very strong hand. Flashlight (the Button) raised pre-flop and I called. I am first to act. Checking is the standard play here to allow the pre-flop aggressor to continue with their range (a continuation bet), which I can then call or raise. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs and only get called by better hands or similar Queens. I will check to see how the Button reacts.

The flop is relatively dry, providing few immediate threats to my Ace-high hand. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a classic spot to continuation bet to maintain initiative and capitalize on the likelihood that the Big Blind missed this board. Betting 300 into the 650 pot puts pressure on my opponent and could win the pot immediately if they hold weak cards.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen on a dry board (Qs-8d-4c). After I checked, Flashlight fired a continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 650. This is a standard sized bet from the pre-flop aggressor. While my kicker is weak (3d), top pair in a heads-up pot against a Button open is far too strong to fold. Raising here might scare off their bluffs or weaker hands (like pocket pairs or 8x), whereas calling allows them to continue bluffing on future streets or keep their range wide. My goal is to extract value from their range while keeping the pot manageable given my kicker. I will call the 300.

On the turn, the 6 of diamonds is a favorable card for me as it gives me a flush draw to go along with my top pair of Queens. I am currently out of position against Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor. Checking here is the best strategic move to control the size of the pot and allow Flashlight to continue betting with a wider range of hands, including bluffs or weaker pairs. I am not afraid of many cards on the river, and my hand is too strong to fold but potentially outkicked if I face heavy aggression. By checking, I maintain flexibility and can comfortably call a bet to see the river.

I am in position on the turn with Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). My opponent checked to me, which signals they might be check-calling or possibly trapping with a Queen or better. Given the board texture (8d, 4c, Qs, 6d), I have limited showdown value and my gutshot draw has some equity, but not enough to justify a large bet. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially see a free river card, which is the most strategic play here.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (Q-3). The board is relatively safe, though the Ten on the river is a card that could have improved some of Flashlight's marginal holdings like JT or T9. On the turn, the action went check-check, which suggests Flashlight likely has a marginal made hand (like an 8 or a middle pocket pair) or is giving up on a bluff. By checking the river, I am playing for showdown value. I don't expect many worse hands to call a bet if I lead out, but checking allows Flashlight to potentially bluff with missed draws or check back with hands that I beat. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but given the turn check, I am likely ahead and looking to get to showdown cheaply.

I have reached the river with Ace-high on a board of 8d-4c-Qs-6d-Tc. My opponent has checked to me. My hand has no showdown value against any pair, but betting would only be called by better hands. Checking back is the standard play to minimize losses and potentially win if my opponent is also playing air.
I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand with negative expected value. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand. Since the Button has folded, it's just me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Flashlight has a stack of 5,100 chips (51 BB). While I could attempt to steal the blind with a raise, Jack-Five does not play well post-flop if called, and I'd be out of position. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While it is standard to open a wide range from the button in a three-handed game to steal the blinds, this hand is at the very bottom of the range and does not play well post-flop if called. Given that Pro is the chip leader and has been active in defending his blinds, and Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and may look to jam over an opening raise, folding is the most disciplined and low-risk play to preserve my current stack.

With the button folding, I am heads-up against the big blind. King-high is a reasonably strong hand in this blind-versus-blind scenario. Rather than opening with a raise that might be 3-bet by the big blind, I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply and maintain pot control.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (8s 5c). The Small Blind simply limped in. There is no reason to risk chips by raising here as a bluff. Checking allows me to see a free flop and evaluate from there.

The flop is Ace-high, which heavily favors the Big Blind's checking range. With only King-high, I am behind any Ace, any Ten, or any pocket pair. I should check to the Big Blind to pot control and re-evaluate based on their action, as betting into an Ace-high board with King-high is unlikely to generate folds from an opponent's reasonable calling range.

I have 8-high with no pair and no draw on an Ace-high flop. Flashlight checked to me, but there's no reason to risk chips on a bluff here when I have zero equity and the board heavily favors hands that would limp pre-flop (like weak Aces or Tens). Checking behind is the safest and most standard play.

I have improved to a pair of Kings on the turn. However, the board features an Ace, which is squarely in the Big Blind's checking range and likely improves their holding. Betting now would likely only be called by an Ace or a better King, causing me to play a bloated pot with a marginal hand. Checking allows me to maintain pot control and evaluate the opponent's action. If the opponent checks back, I get a free look at the river; if they bet, I can make a decision based on the size of the bet, as I have decent showdown value.

I have 8-high with no draw on a very connected, high-card-heavy board. The pot is minimal, and my hand has zero equity or showdown value. Checking again is the most prudent action as there's no reason to attempt a bluff here against an opponent whose limping range likely connects with these broadway cards.

I have reached the river with a pair of Kings (Kh on the board with the Ks). The board is Ace-high (A-K-T-4-2). Since the action has been checked down to this point, it is highly likely that neither player has a premium hand, or the opponent is trapping. With only a pair of Kings, I lose to any Ace, but I beat any lower pair or busted draw. Betting is ineffective because I would likely only be called by an Ace, which has me beat, or get a fold from worse hands. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown and minimize losses against a potential Ace, while maximizing the chance that my pair of Kings remains the best hand.

I have 8-high with absolutely zero showdown value on a board filled with broadway cards and a flush possible. The pot is extremely small (2 BB), and there's no strategic merit in trying to bluff at this point, as Flashlight's limp and check-down range likely still includes hands with some showdown value (weak pairs, higher high-cards). Checking to see the showdown and conceding this negligible pot is the optimal decision.

9-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very little potential to improve or win a showdown. In a three-way pot, paying the full 100 chips to see a flop is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2d, a very weak suited connector. Flashlight (Button) folded. Folding is the most disciplined action to preserve my stack.
With A9o on the Button 3-handed, I have a strong starting hand that plays well in position. A standard raise to 3 big blinds applies pressure on the blinds and gives me the initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with Seven of Spades and Deuce of Spades. This is one of the weakest hands in poker, even with the flush potential. Pro (Button) has opened for 300. Calling out of position with such a weak hand against a chip leader or near-equal stack is mathematically losing in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand (Jack of Diamonds, Five of Diamonds) facing an open raise from the Button. Given I am out of position and the hand strength is marginal, calling is not mathematically sound against an aggressive opponent's opening range. Preserving my stack for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

I'm on the button with a strong suited connector (Tc Jc). I will open for a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. This hand has good playability and I will have positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Five offsuit against a button raise. I will call to see a flop in position, keeping the pot manageable, although I must be prepared to fold if the Big Blind squeezes or if I miss the flop completely.

With KQo in the Big Blind facing a Button raise and a Small Blind call, I have a strong hand that benefits from a squeeze. 3-betting will often fold out the Small Blind's capped range and put pressure on the Button's wide opening range. Squeezing to 1200 charges them to see a flop and often wins the pot pre-flop.

I have a strong suited connector (Tc Jc) on the button. Pro has 3-bet to 1200 from the big blind after I raised and Flashlight called. Calling 900 more gives me roughly 3:1 pot odds (calling 900 to win a pot of 2700 if Flashlight folds, or more if he calls). Given my position and the high playability of my hand, calling is the most profitable play. It allows me to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage against Pro, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands.

The pot is currently 2700, and I need to call 900 to see a flop. With A5o, I have decent raw equity and an Ace blocker, and the pot odds of 3:1 make this a profitable call despite being out of position. I will call to see if I can hit the flop or an Ace, and re-evaluate my options on the next street.

The flop is 8h-10d-4c, and I am first to act out of position in a three-way pot. My hand, Ac-5h, has completely whiffed the board, providing me with only Ace-high and no meaningful draws. Betting into two opponents on this board is not optimal. Checking is the standard play to see how the other players respond and to keep the pot size manageable while I evaluate their strength.

After squeezing pre-flop and getting called by both opponents, the flop comes 8-T-4 rainbow. I missed completely with KQ high, and this board connects well with the middling pairs and suited connectors that would call a 3-bet. C-betting into two players here with no strong draw is risky, especially given Flashlight's short stack (SPR is near 1). Checking is the safest and most standard play to control the pot and evaluate the action.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a relatively dry board (8h Td 4c). Pre-flop, Pro 3-bet and both Flashlight and I called, creating a large pot. Both opponents have checked on the flop. While I likely have the best hand, betting into two opponents in a 3-bet pot with a medium-strength hand like top pair can be risky. Checking back serves several purposes: it controls the pot size, protects my range, and avoids a potential check-raise from a trapping hand (like AA, KK, or a set). It also allows my opponents to potentially bluff on the turn or call a smaller bet later with weaker hands. I will check to see a turn card for free and reassess based on the next action.

The turn card 5c improved my hand to a pair of 5s, but this remains a very weak holding in a three-way pot. The board is quite connected with potential straight draws like 6-7, 7-9, and overcards, making my pair extremely vulnerable. Since I am acting first and have little showdown value, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and evaluate my opponents' intentions. If faced with a significant bet, I will likely fold, as I cannot profitably continue with such a low pair.

I have KQ high with no pair and no draw on an 8-T-4-5 board. After a multi-way 3-bet pot where both opponents called pre-flop and the flop checked through, I still have very little equity. Betting into two opponents with no direct draw or pair is risky, especially given Flashlight's short stack which would make any bet highly committing. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot.

I have top pair (tens) with a decent kicker and have now picked up a flush draw on the turn.
