With the pot at 3600, I should bet for value and to protect my hand against straight draws (67, 97) or overcards that might be checking. A bet of 1500 is roughly 40% of the pot, which is enough to extract value from weaker pairs or draws while keeping the pot manageable if an opponent is trapping.

The board is 8h-Td-4c-5c. My hand is Ac-5h, giving me a pair of 5s. I am first to act out of position in a three-way pot, facing a bet of 1500 into a 3600 pot. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my remaining chips for a more favorable spot.

With King-Queen high on an 8-10-4-5 board and facing a turn bet of 1500 from the pre-flop raiser in a 3-bet pot, my hand has virtually no equity. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Calling or bluff-raising here would be reckless and burning chips. Folding is the only sensible decision.

I am the shortest stack at the table and currently in the button position. With K8o, I have a decent enough hand to see a flop in position against the blinds. Calling the 100 chips allows me to evaluate the flop while keeping my stack intact, looking for a favorable board to potentially make a move or find a better spot given my limited chip count.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, 4c 3h, and face a limp from the Button. Completing the bet out of position with such a poor holding is unprofitable, so the optimal play is to simply fold and save the chips.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 3c. Flashlight on the Button has limped into the pot, and the Small Blind (Pro) has folded. Since the pot is already closed if I check, and my hand is extremely weak, there is no merit in raising. I will take the free flop to see if I can improve.

I have flopped middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry board in a limped pot. In this situation, my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to value bet for multiple streets. By checking, I keep the pot small, allow Flashlight to potentially bluff with weaker hands, and maintain pot control. If Flashlight bets, I intend to call most standard-sized bets to see a turn card.

I am the shortest stack and have missed the flop completely with King-high. The pot is small and checking behind allows me to see the turn for free, conserving my limited stack while waiting for a better spot to accumulate chips or finding a chance to bluff if the turn card is favorable.

On the turn, an Ace appears on the board. I still have second pair (sevens), but the Ace is a very likely card for Flashlight to have hit or to use as a bluffing card. However, they checked back the turn, which suggests they might not have an Ace or a strong Nine. Since the pot is small (250) and I have some showdown value, I will check again to keep the pot controlled and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff that I might consider calling on the river.

With only King-high on an Ace-high board, I have no real showdown value and a bluff is unnecessary in this small pot. Checking behind is the most efficient play to control the pot size and see the river card for free, minimizing risk while maintaining my stack.

I have backed into trips (Sevens) on the river in a limped pot. The board is 7s-2c-9h-As-7c. Flashlight has checked every street so far, suggesting they do not have a strong hand like an Ace or a Nine. Since I have the best hand almost certainly, I will bet for value to extract some chips from any pair or high-card hand Flashlight might be holding. A bet of 200 into a 250 pot is a standard value sizing to get called by a Nine or a pocket pair.

With King-high on a board of 7-2-9-A-7, I have no showdown value. Flash checked the flop and turn, suggesting a weak range, but a river bet into me usually indicates they have finally hit a pair, such as an Ace or a 7, or are attempting a thin bluff. Given my short stack status, calling a bet with King-high is not a profitable move. Folding preserves my remaining chips for a higher-percentage spot.

6c 4c is a suited one-gapper, which is highly speculative. The Small Blind is also deep-stacked and can defend aggressively. Folding is a safe, zero-variance decision to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position or facing a 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 10-4 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded. Flashlight, the Big Blind, has a relatively short stack of 3,850. While it only costs 50 more to complete the bet, 10-4 is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. I am on the button with Q2s. This is a very weak hand. Given my current chip lead and the fact that Flashlight is short-stacked and might be looking to shove over a raise, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.
I am in the small blind with a suited gapper (Ts, 3s). With 50 chips to call and 150 in the pot, the pot odds justify completing the bet to see a flop cheaply in a blind-versus-blind situation.

I have 9h 2h in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight) just limped. My hand is too weak to isolate with a raise, but since I'm already in the Big Blind, I can simply check and see a free flop. There's no reason to risk extra chips with a 9-high hand right now.

The flop is monotone with three diamonds (Qd, Jd, 6d). I hold the Ten and Three of Spades, giving me no pair and no diamond to contest the flush. I am out of position and have no equity. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses.

I have 9-high with no diamonds on a monotone Q-J-6 diamond board. My opponent checked, and I have absolutely no equity or viable bluffing blockers to attempt to steal the pot here. Checking is the only sensible option.

The board (Qd, Jd, 6d, 7s) provides no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw for my hand (Ts, 3s). The turn action was checked through, and there is no reason to initiate a bluff in a blind-versus-blind pot where my range is capped and I am out of position. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain a conservative strategy and potentially see a showdown if the river is favorable, or fold if forced to against a significant bet.

My opponent checked again on the turn. I still have 9-high with absolutely no draws or meaningful equity on this monotone diamond board. Given the small pot and lack of blockers, there is no reason to attempt a bluff. Checking to see a free river is the best decision.

The board is finalized with a flush and a pair on the board (Qd, Jd, 6d, 7s, 7d). My hand (Ts, 3s) has no showdown value whatsoever, and the action has been checked through on all streets. Betting would be a pointless bluff against a range that could easily contain a diamond or a pair, and checking is the standard play to see the showdown for free in this position.

The board has four diamonds and pairs the sevens. I hold 9-high with no diamonds, meaning I am playing the board with a 9 kicker, which loses to almost any two cards my opponent could hold (Ace-high, King-high, any pair, or any diamond). Since the pot is minimal (200) and I have no credible blockers to represent a strong flush, attempting a bluff here is unnecessary and risky. Checking and conceding the small pot is the most optimal decision.

I am in the button position with 6s 8c, which is a weak hand in a three-handed game. With a stack of 4100 (41 big blinds), I need to play relatively disciplined to find a better spot to double up against the significantly larger stacks of my opponents. Calling with 6-8 offsuit is not profitable in this situation, so I will fold.

With King-Three offsuit in the Small Blind, I have a very marginal and disconnected hand. Playing out of position against the Big Blind (who also happens to be the chip leader) with such a weak hand is generally a losing play over time. I will just fold and look for a better spot.
I am on the Button with a very weak hand, 2s 6s (suited but low and disconnected). There is no value in playing this hand, so the optimal choice is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. After the Button folds, I am heads-up against Flashlight in the Big Blind, who has a shorter stack of 4,000 chips. Ace-Nine is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, and I will maintain my standard opening size of 300 chips to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot pre-flop or play the hand with the range advantage.

My hand, 4-2 offsuit, is extremely weak and provides poor playability post-flop. As I am out of position against an aggressive opponent, defending the big blind here would be a statistically losing play. Folding is the optimal strategy.

I am on the button with Jd 8h. This is a marginal hand, but in a three-handed game, it's often worth opening or defending from the button. My current stack is 14,100, which is the largest at the table. To my left are Flashlight (3,950 chips) and Pro (11,800 chips). In previous hands, I have frequently raised to 300 from the button to pick up blinds or take control of the pot. Given the stack depths and the tendency for small blind/big blind to fold to pressure, I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive image and try to win the pot pre-flop.

My hand, Ten of Hearts and Six of Spades, is too weak to defend the small blind against a button raise. Being out of position, playing T6 offsuit against a strong range is mathematically unfavorable. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Facing a 3x open from the Button. While the Button might be opening a wide range, Q7o is too weak and disconnected to play profitably out of position. It has poor equity and negative implied odds if we hit a marginal pair. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

I am holding 9s 8s on the button, which is a strong suited connector. With a stack size of 3950, I am currently the short stack at the table. To increase my chip count, I need to play aggressively and take the initiative when in a favorable position. Raising to 300 allows me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or see a flop with the initiative.

I have Pocket Kings in the Small Blind facing a Button open. This is a premium hand, and the standard play is to 3-bet to build the pot and extract value. Flashlight has a relatively short stack, so a 3-bet to around 1000 puts significant pressure on him and might induce a shove from worse pairs or strong Aces.

I am holding Queen-Six of spades in the Big Blind. Flashlight opened from the Button, and Pro 3-bet to 1000 from the Small Blind. While Q6s has some playability, it is significantly behind a typical 3-bet range from the Small Blind. Given the action and the fact that I would be playing a bloated pot with a marginal hand, I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

With 9s8s on the button, I'm facing a significant 3-bet from the small blind (Pro). As the short stack, I need to accumulate chips to stay competitive. While folding is safe, calling allows me to play in position with a strong suited connector, and my remaining stack size provides a manageable SPR for post-flop play. This call is calculated to exploit my position despite the 3-bet.

With pocket Kings on a low, somewhat connected flop (6h-2h-5s), we have an overpair that is highly likely to be the best hand but vulnerable to straight and flush draws. Flashlight has an SPR of about 1.4 (2950 behind into a pot of 2100). Betting around half the pot (1000) is ideal here. It charges draws like hearts or straight draws to see the turn, extracts value from mid-pocket pairs (like 77-QQ) or a stubbornly played 6x, and perfectly sets up an easy all-in on the turn. We are fully committed and happily calling if he decides to jam over this bet.

I am facing a continuation bet on a board of 6h-2h-5s, and I hold 9s-8s. This provides me with an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or a 4). With my current stack of 2950, after calling the 1000, I will have 1950 remaining, creating a very low SPR. Given the strength of my draw and the likelihood of hitting my straight or potentially having fold equity if I shove later, calling is the most strategic move to keep the hand alive while maintaining a manageable pot size. Raising all-in now is also a viable option to maximize pressure, but calling allows me to see the turn before committing the rest of my stack.

The turn card is the Ten of Spades. While it brings a second flush draw and connects with some straight draws, our pocket Kings remain an extremely strong overpair. Given the very low SPR (Flashlight has 1950 remaining into a 4100 pot), we are completely pot-committed. The optimal play is to put his remaining stack in the middle to extract value from worse pairs (like AT, JJ, QQ) and charge any draws heavily. I am dealt pocket fives on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a relatively strong starting hand that plays well in position. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take the initiative, build the pot, and pressure my opponent's blind.

The board is highly coordinated (9-K-J-8-3 with three hearts), offering numerous straight and flush possibilities. Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even heads-up on the button. It plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify an open-raise or a call. Folding is the most profitable long-term decision here.

Pro called my flop bet on a very dry board, which suggests he either has a piece of it (an Ace), a draw (hearts), or a mid-pair. By checking the turn, I control the pot size and avoid over-committing against a potential full house while also keeping his bluffs and weaker draws in the pot for a possible showdown or river decision.
